region_groups.name: Midwest
region_groups.description: The Great Lakes and Corn Belt heartland—rich soils, continental seasons, and national-scale production of corn, soybeans, and dairy.

regions.name: Ohio
regions.description: Ohio’s lake-moderated north and warmer south support sweet corn, tomatoes, apples, and significant soybean and corn acreage.

regions.name: Indiana
regions.description: Indiana is classic Corn Belt—tomatoes and melons in summer, pumpkins and apples in fall, with consistent continental seasonality.

regions.name: Illinois
regions.description: Illinois leads in soybeans and produces vast corn; specialty farms near cities supply summer vegetables and fall squash.

regions.name: Michigan
regions.description: Michigan’s lakes moderate temperatures—nation-leading tart cherries, blueberries, apples, and asparagus with cool-climate fruit intensity.

regions.name: Wisconsin
regions.description: Wisconsin is America’s dairy core with strong snap beans, potatoes, cranberries, and ginseng—short warm season, crisp falls.

regions.name: Minnesota
regions.description: Minnesota’s cold winters and warm summers support sugar beets, peas, sweet corn, and wild rice—local season starts later than southern Midwest.

regions.name: Iowa
regions.description: Iowa anchors corn and soy production; farmers’ markets still overflow with sweet corn, melons, and tomatoes in peak summer.

regions.name: Missouri
regions.description: Missouri bridges upper South and Midwest—soybeans, rice in the bootheel, Ozark orchards, and long summer vegetable markets.
